book.name: 1 Chronicles
book.author: Unknown (traditionally attributed to Ezra; often called 'the Chronicler')
book.dateWritten: c. 450–400 BC (post-exilic period, after the return from Babylon)
book.writtenFrom: Jerusalem, after the return from Babylonian exile
book.writtenTo: The restored Jewish community in Jerusalem -- providing them with a theological history that emphasizes the temple, worship, and the Davidic covenant as the foundation for rebuilding their national identity

explainer.overview: 1 Chronicles retells Israel's history from Adam to David, but with a distinctive perspective shaped by the post-exilic community's needs. The first nine chapters contain genealogies that establish the continuity of God's people from creation through the exile and return. The remaining chapters focus almost exclusively on David's reign, particularly his preparations for the temple and organization of worship. Unlike 2 Samuel, which honestly portrays David's failures, Chronicles presents an idealized David focused on worship -- not because the author was unaware of David's sins, but because the returned exiles needed a model for building a worshipping community.
explainer.historicalContext: Written after the exile (c. 450-400 BC), 1 Chronicles addresses a community that had returned to a devastated Jerusalem and was struggling to rebuild the temple, re-establish worship, and understand their identity. They needed to know they were still God's covenant people with a continuous heritage stretching back to Adam. The genealogies are not mere lists but theological statements of identity and continuity.
explainer.whyItMatters: 1 Chronicles establishes that Israel's identity is fundamentally about worship, not political power. David's greatest achievement is not military conquest but preparing for the temple. The book models a community centered on the presence of God, organized worship, and faithful giving -- exactly what the returned exiles needed to build. It shows that even after catastrophic exile, the covenant community can be rebuilt around the practices of worship.
keyThemes: Genealogical continuity from Adam through the exile
Temple preparation as David's central legacy
The Davidic covenant as the basis for hope
God's sovereignty in choosing leaders and establishing worship
The importance of seeking God with whole heart

people.name: David
people.role: King of Israel, founder of the dynasty, worship organizer
people.bio: In Chronicles, David is presented primarily as the visionary who conceived, planned, funded, and organized everything for the temple -- even though he was not allowed to build it. His military conquests provide the wealth and security for temple building, his musical gifts organize the worship, and his final public speech provides the theological vision. Chronicles largely omits David's personal failures (Bathsheba, Amnon, Absalom) to focus on his role as the model for the post-exilic community.
people.culturalContext: The Chronicler's David is not a whitewash but a selective portrait for a specific audience. The returned exiles knew the stories of David's sins from Samuel. What they needed from Chronicles was a model for how to organize a community centered on worship -- and that model was David.

people.name: Solomon
people.role: David's son who will build the temple
people.bio: In 1 Chronicles, Solomon appears primarily as the recipient of David's preparations. He is the one chosen by God to build the temple, and David's final chapters are devoted to ensuring Solomon has everything he needs: plans, materials, workers, and spiritual encouragement. Solomon's later failures are not mentioned in 1 Chronicles.
people.culturalContext: Solomon represents the next generation that will inherit and complete what the current generation prepares. For the post-exilic community, this pattern was directly relevant: they were laying foundations that future generations would build upon.

people.name: Joab
people.bio: Joab appears in Chronicles primarily in military contexts, leading David's armies against the Ammonites and other enemies. The Chronicler omits most of Joab's darker actions recorded in Samuel (killing Abner, Absalom, and Amasa) to maintain focus on the positive military achievements that secured wealth and territory for the temple project.
people.culturalContext: The military victories led by Joab provided the material resources and political stability that made temple preparations possible.

places.name: Jerusalem / Zion
places.description: The city David captured from the Jebusites and made his capital. In Chronicles, Jerusalem is primarily significant as the future site of the temple -- the place God chose for His name to dwell.
places.culturalSignificance: For the post-exilic community rebuilding Jerusalem, Chronicles established the city's theological significance: it was chosen by God before it was built by humans. Jerusalem was not merely a political capital but the divinely designated center of worship.

places.name: The Threshing Floor of Ornan (Araunah)
places.description: The specific site where David built an altar to stop the plague after the census, and which became the location of Solomon's temple. David purchased it from Ornan the Jebusite for the full price.
places.culturalSignificance: The identification of this specific location as the future temple site is one of Chronicles' most important contributions. It connects David's act of repentance and worship with the permanent establishment of God's dwelling place.

places.name: Hebron
places.description: The city where David first reigned for seven years before capturing Jerusalem. In Chronicles, Hebron is where all Israel gathers to make David king, emphasizing national unity under the Davidic dynasty.
places.culturalSignificance: Hebron represented the beginning of David's united kingship. Chronicles emphasizes that all twelve tribes came to Hebron to crown David, presenting an idealized picture of national unity.

timeline.event: Genealogies from Adam through the tribal period (chapters 1-9)
Saul's death; David becomes king at Hebron
David captures Jerusalem; makes it his capital
David brings the ark to Jerusalem
God's covenant with David; the Davidic promise
David's wars provide wealth for the temple
David's census and the plague; threshing floor purchased
David's final preparations and charge to Solomon

explainer.summary: A sweeping genealogy from Adam to Abraham, then through Abraham's sons (Ishmael, Isaac), and Esau's descendants (Edom). This chapter places Israel's story within universal human history, showing that the chosen line is part of a larger human family stretching back to creation.
themes: Universal human history leading to Israel
The chosen line within the broader family of nations
Continuity from creation to the post-exilic community
explainer.contextInBook: The genealogies establish identity for the returned exiles. They needed to know they were connected to Adam through an unbroken line, that their God was the God of all creation, and that their specific calling emerged from within the universal human story.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Both a personal name and the Hebrew word for 'huma
wordStudies.meaning: Both a personal name and the Hebrew word for 'humanity.' Beginning with Adam establishes that Israel's story is part of the human story from the very beginning -- God's purposes for Israel serve His purposes for all humanity.

Generations or genealogical records. The structuring principle of Genesis and Chronicles alike, 'toledot' traces the transmission of identity, promise, and responsibility from one generation to the next.

verses.text: Adam, Seth, Enosh,
verses.explainer: Adam, Seth, Enosh -- the genealogy begins at the very beginning of human history, connecting Israel to the creation of humanity itself.

crossReferences.connection: The source genealogy from Adam to Noah that Chronicles condenses

verses.text: Kenan, Mahalalel, Jared,
verses.explainer: Kenan, Mahalalel, Jared -- the pre-flood patriarchs listed without narrative detail, their very names carrying the memory of humanity's earliest generations.

verses.text: Enoch, Methuselah, Lamech,
verses.explainer: Enoch, Methuselah, Lamech -- Enoch who walked with God and Methuselah the longest-lived man, compressed into a single genealogical line.

crossReferences.connection: The fuller accounts of Enoch's walk with God and Methuselah's extraordinary lifespan

verses.text: Noah, Shem, Ham, and Japheth.
verses.explainer: Noah, Shem, Ham, and Japheth -- the turning point: from Noah, all post-flood humanity descends. The three sons become the ancestors of three great family groups.

thenVsNow.then: The genealogy moves quickly through catastrophic events (the flood) to show that God's purposes continue through judgment, not despite it -- a message particularly relevant to post-exilic Jews.
thenVsNow.now: The genealogy moves quickly through catastrophic events (the flood) to show that God's purposes continue through judgment, not despite it -- a message particularly relevant to post-exilic Jews.

verses.text: The sons of Japheth: Gomer, Magog, Madai, Javan, Tubal, Meshech, and Tiras.
verses.explainer: The sons of Japheth: Gomer, Magog, Madai, Javan, Tubal, Meshech, and Tiras -- the peoples of Asia Minor, Europe, and the Mediterranean world.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The Table of Nations in Genesis 10 provides the background for these names. The Chronicler includes the non-Israelite lines to show awareness of the broader human family within which Israel exists.

verses.text: The sons of Gomer: Ashkenaz, Diphath, and Togarmah.
verses.explainer: The sons of Gomer and Javan -- tracing the branches of the Japhethite line into specific peoples known to the post-exilic community.

verses.text: The sons of Javan: Elishah, Tarshish, Kittim, and Rodanim.
verses.explainer: The sons of Javan: Elishah, Tarshish, Kittim, and Rodanim -- maritime peoples of the Mediterranean, known to the post-exilic community through trade.

verses.text: The sons of Ham: Cush, Mizraim, Put, and Canaan.
verses.explainer: The sons of Ham: Cush, Egypt, Put, and Canaan -- the African and Canaanite peoples, including Israel's most significant neighbors.

verses.text: The sons of Cush: Seba, Havilah, Sabta, Raama, Sabteca. The sons of Raamah: Sheba and Dedan.
verses.explainer: The sons of Cush -- the Ethiopian/Nubian lineage branching into various peoples.

verses.text: Cush became the father of Nimrod. He began to be a mighty one in the earth.
verses.explainer: Cush fathered Nimrod, the first mighty man on earth -- the only narrative detail in this rapid genealogy, highlighting Nimrod's exceptional significance.

crossReferences.connection: The fuller account of Nimrod as founder of Babylon and Assyrian cities

verses.text: Mizraim became the father of Ludim, Anamim, Lehabim, Naphtuhim,
verses.explainer: Egypt fathered Ludim, Anamim, Lehabim, Naphtuhim -- Egyptian-related peoples of North Africa.

verses.text: Pathrusim, Casluhim (where the Philistines came from), and Caphtorim.
verses.explainer: Pathrusim, Casluhim (from whom the Philistines came), and Caphtorim -- the Philistine origin is noted because of their significance in Israelite history.

verses.text: Canaan became the father of Sidon his firstborn, Heth,
verses.explainer: Canaan fathered Sidon his firstborn and Heth -- the progenitors of the Phoenician and Hittite peoples who inhabited the promised land before Israel.

verses.text: the Jebusite, and the Amorite, the Girgashite,
verses.explainer: The Jebusites, Amorites, Girgashites -- Canaanite peoples who occupied the land before Israel's conquest.

verses.text: the Hivite, the Arkite, the Sinite,
verses.explainer: Hivites, Arkites, Sinites -- additional Canaanite peoples, establishing the comprehensive list of nations God displaced for Israel.

verses.text: the Arvadite, the Zemarite, and the Hamathite.
verses.explainer: Arvadites, Zemarites, and Hamathites -- the northern Canaanite peoples extending into modern Lebanon and Syria.

verses.text: The sons of Shem: Elam, Asshur, Arpachshad, Lud, Aram, Uz, Hul, Gether, and Meshech.
verses.explainer: The sons of Shem: Elam, Asshur, Arpachshad, Lud, and Aram -- the Semitic peoples, the line through which Israel's ancestors will emerge.

verses.text: Arpachshad became the father of Shelah, and Shelah became the father of Eber.
verses.explainer: And the sons of Aram -- further branches of the Semitic family.

verses.text: To Eber were born two sons: the name of the one was Peleg, for in his days the earth was divided; and his brother’s name was Joktan.
verses.explainer: Arpachshad fathered Shelah, and Shelah fathered Eber -- the line narrowing toward Abraham through Eber, from whose name 'Hebrew' derives.

verses.text: Joktan became the father of Almodad, Sheleph, Hazarmaveth, Jerah,
verses.explainer: Eber's sons Peleg and Joktan -- Peleg's name means 'division,' marking the dispersal of peoples.

verses.text: Hadoram, Uzal, Diklah,
verses.explainer: Joktan's descendants -- the Arabian peoples branching off from the chosen line.

verses.text: Ebal, Abimael, Sheba,
verses.explainer: More of Joktan's sons -- Arabian tribal groups.

verses.text: Ophir, Havilah, and Jobab. All these were the sons of Joktan.
verses.explainer: Further sons of Joktan -- the genealogy traces branches before returning to the main line.

verses.text: Shem, Arpachshad, Shelah,
verses.explainer: Shem, Arpachshad, Shelah -- the direct line from Shem to Abraham is now traced without branches, focusing on the chosen lineage.

verses.text: Eber, Peleg, Reu,
verses.explainer: Eber, Peleg, Reu -- the line continuing toward Abraham.

verses.text: Serug, Nahor, Terah,
verses.explainer: Serug, Nahor, Terah -- Abraham's grandfather, great-grandfather, and father.

verses.text: Abram (also called Abraham).
verses.explainer: Abram, that is, Abraham -- the culmination of the universal genealogy: from all of humanity, one man is chosen through whom all nations will be blessed.

crossReferences.connection: God's call to Abraham and the promise to bless all nations through him

thenVsNow.then: The genealogical journey from Adam to Abraham establishes that God's choice of one man and one people serves His purpose for all humanity.
thenVsNow.now: The genealogical journey from Adam to Abraham establishes that God's choice of one man and one people serves His purpose for all humanity.

verses.text: The sons of Abraham: Isaac and Ishmael.
verses.explainer: The sons of Abraham: Isaac and Ishmael -- both sons listed, but the chosen line will continue through Isaac.

verses.text: These are their generations: the firstborn of Ishmael, Nebaioth; then Kedar, Adbeel, Mibsam,
verses.explainer: The firstborn of Ishmael: Nebaioth, then Kedar, Adbeel, Mibsam -- Ishmael's twelve sons, fulfilling God's promise that Ishmael too would become a great nation.

crossReferences.connection: God's promise that Ishmael would father twelve princes and become a great nation

verses.text: Mishma, Dumah, Massa, Hadad, Tema,
verses.explainer: Mishma, Dumah, Massa, Hadad, Tema -- more of Ishmael's sons, Arabian tribal ancestors.

verses.text: Jetur, Naphish, and Kedemah. These are the sons of Ishmael.
verses.explainer: Jetur, Naphish, and Kedemah -- the complete list of Ishmael's twelve sons. The non-chosen line is recorded with respect.

verses.text: The sons of Keturah, Abraham’s concubine: she bore Zimran, Jokshan, Medan, Midian, Ishbak, and Shuah. The sons of Jokshan: Sheba and Dedan.
verses.explainer: The sons of Keturah, Abraham's concubine -- Abraham's other descendants through his later wife Keturah.

verses.text: The sons of Midian: Ephah, Epher, Hanoch, Abida, and Eldaah. All these were the sons of Keturah.
verses.explainer: The sons of Midian and their descendants -- Midianite peoples, significant in later Israelite history through Moses' father-in-law and the Midianite conflicts.

verses.text: Abraham became the father of Isaac. The sons of Isaac: Esau and Israel.
verses.explainer: Abraham fathered Isaac. Isaac's sons: Esau and Israel -- the genealogy arrives at the two brothers whose descendants will define the narrative.

verses.text: The sons of Esau: Eliphaz, Reuel, Jeush, Jalam, and Korah.
verses.explainer: The sons of Esau: Eliphaz, Reuel, Jeush, Jalam, and Korah -- Esau's line is traced first before returning to Israel's line in chapter 2.

verses.text: The sons of Eliphaz: Teman, Omar, Zephi, Gatam, Kenaz, Timna, and Amalek.
verses.explainer: The sons of Eliphaz -- Esau's grandsons through his firstborn, including Amalek, Israel's persistent enemy.

verses.text: The sons of Reuel: Nahath, Zerah, Shammah, and Mizzah.
verses.explainer: The sons of Reuel -- more of Esau's descendants.

verses.text: The sons of Seir: Lotan, Shobal, Zibeon, Anah, Dishon, Ezer, and Dishan.
verses.explainer: The sons of Seir -- the original inhabitants of Edom's territory, whose genealogy is included because they intermingled with Esau's descendants.

verses.text: The sons of Lotan: Hori and Homam; and Timna was Lotan’s sister.
verses.explainer: Lotan's sons and his sister Timna -- Seir's descendants who merged with the Edomite line.

verses.text: The sons of Shobal: Alian, Manahath, Ebal, Shephi, and Onam. The sons of Zibeon: Aiah and Anah.
verses.explainer: The sons of Shobal and Zibeon.

verses.text: The son of Anah: Dishon. The sons of Dishon: Hamran, Eshban, Ithran, and Cheran.
verses.explainer: Anah's son Dishon, and the sons of Dishon.

verses.text: The sons of Ezer: Bilhan, Zaavan, and Jaakan. The sons of Dishan: Uz and Aran.
verses.explainer: The sons of Ezer and Dishan -- completing the Seirite genealogy.

verses.text: Now these are the kings who reigned in the land of Edom, before any king reigned over the children of Israel: Bela the son of Beor; and the name of his city was Dinhabah.
verses.explainer: The kings who reigned in Edom before any king reigned over Israel -- a list of Edomite kings, noting that Edom had a monarchy before Israel did.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The list of Edomite kings demonstrates that advanced political organization was not what made Israel special. Edom had kings generations before Israel, yet Israel received the covenant.

verses.text: Bela died, and Jobab the son of Zerah of Bozrah reigned in his place.
verses.explainer: Bela son of Beor reigned in Edom -- the first Edomite king.

verses.text: Jobab died, and Husham of the land of the Temanites reigned in his place.
verses.explainer: Jobab succeeded him -- the Edomite succession continued.

verses.text: Husham died, and Hadad the son of Bedad, who struck Midian in the field of Moab, reigned in his place; and the name of his city was Avith.
verses.explainer: Husham succeeded, then Hadad who defeated Midian in Moab's territory.

verses.text: Hadad died, and Samlah of Masrekah reigned in his place.
verses.explainer: Samlah of Masrekah succeeded Hadad.

verses.text: Samlah died, and Shaul of Rehoboth by the River reigned in his place.
verses.explainer: Shaul of Rehoboth on the Euphrates succeeded Samlah.

verses.text: Shaul died, and Baal Hanan the son of Achbor reigned in his place.
verses.explainer: Baal-hanan son of Achbor succeeded Shaul.

verses.text: Baal Hanan died, and Hadad reigned in his place; and the name of his city was Pai: and his wife’s name was Mehetabel, the daughter of Matred, the daughter of Mezahab.
verses.explainer: Hadad succeeded Baal-hanan; his city was Pai.

verses.text: Then Hadad died. The chiefs of Edom were: chief Timna, chief Aliah, chief Jetheth,
verses.explainer: The chiefs of Edom: Timna, Alvah, Jetheth -- Edomite clan leaders after the kings.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The shift from kings to chiefs may indicate a change in Edom's political structure, or it may list concurrent local rulers rather than a sequential monarchy.

verses.text: chief Oholibamah, chief Elah, chief Pinon,
verses.explainer: Oholibamah, Elah, Pinon -- more Edomite chiefs.

verses.text: chief Kenaz, chief Teman, chief Mibzar,
verses.explainer: Kenaz, Teman, Mibzar -- Edomite clans known from other biblical and archaeological sources.

verses.text: chief Magdiel, and chief Iram. These are the chiefs of Edom.
verses.explainer: Magdiel and Iram -- the final Edomite chiefs, completing the Esau genealogy before the Chronicler turns to Israel's line.

thenVsNow.then: By recording Edom's genealogy alongside Israel's, the Chronicler acknowledges that Israel exists within a broader family of nations, all descended from Abraham and ultimately from Adam.
thenVsNow.now: By recording Edom's genealogy alongside Israel's, the Chronicler acknowledges that Israel exists within a broader family of nations, all descended from Abraham and ultimately from Adam.

explainer.summary: The genealogy of Israel's twelve sons, then focuses extensively on Judah's line, tracing it through Perez to Hezron and his descendants, including Jesse and David. The detailed treatment of Judah's line reflects the Chronicler's emphasis on the Davidic dynasty as the center of God's purposes.
themes: The genealogical basis for Davidic legitimacy
God's choice working through human generations
explainer.contextInBook: By placing Judah's genealogy first and giving it the most detail, the Chronicler signals that the Davidic line is the theological center of Israel's identity -- not merely a political dynasty but the carrier of God's covenant promise.

wordStudies.meaning: Means 'praise.' Judah receives the most extensive genealogical treatment because from him comes David, and from David comes the promise of eternal kingship. The tribe of praise becomes the royal tribe.
David's father, from Bethlehem. Jesse's genealogy from Perez through Boaz and Ruth establishes the legitimacy of the Davidic line and its connection to the broader story of God's redemptive work.

verses.text: These are the sons of Israel: Reuben, Simeon, Levi, Judah, Issachar, Zebulun,
verses.explainer: The sons of Israel: Reuben, Simeon, Levi, Judah, Issachar, Zebulun -- the twelve sons listed, establishing the tribal framework of the nation.

annotations.word: These are the sons of Israel

verses.text: Dan, Joseph, Benjamin, Naphtali, Gad, and Asher.
verses.explainer: Dan, Joseph, Benjamin, Naphtali, Gad, and Asher -- the complete twelve, establishing that all Israel is one family.

verses.text: The sons of Judah: Er, Onan, and Shelah; which three were born to him of Shua’s daughter the Canaanitess. Er, Judah’s firstborn, was wicked in Yahweh’s sight; and he killed him.
verses.explainer: The sons of Judah: Er, Onan, and Shelah, born to the Canaanite Bath-shua. Er was wicked and God put him to death -- even in the chosen line, unfaithfulness brings judgment.

crossReferences.connection: The full narrative of Judah, Tamar, and the deaths of Er and Onan

verses.text: Tamar his daughter-in-law bore him Perez and Zerah. All the sons of Judah were five.
verses.explainer: Tamar, Judah's daughter-in-law, bore him Perez and Zerah -- Tamar's inclusion is notable, as she is one of the few women named in the genealogies.

crossReferences.connection: Tamar appears in Jesus' genealogy, connecting this line to the Messiah

verses.text: The sons of Perez: Hezron and Hamul.
verses.explainer: The sons of Perez: Hezron and Hamul -- the line through Perez continues to David, making Perez the crucial link.

verses.text: The sons of Zerah: Zimri, Ethan, Heman, Calcol, and Dara; five of them in all.
verses.explainer: The sons of Zerah: Zimri, Ethan, Heman, Calcol, and Dara -- five sons, some known for wisdom (1 Kings 4:31).

crossReferences.connection: Solomon is said to be wiser than Ethan, Heman, Calcol, and Darda -- famous wise men from Zerah's line

verses.text: The son of Carmi: Achar, the troubler of Israel, who committed a trespass in the devoted thing.
verses.explainer: Achan son of Carmi, the troubler of Israel who violated the ban -- the one narrative detail in this section, recalling Achan's sin at Jericho.

crossReferences.connection: The full story of Achan's sin and its consequences

verses.text: The son of Ethan: Azariah.
verses.explainer: Ethan's son Azariah.

verses.text: The sons also of Hezron, who were born to him: Jerahmeel, Ram, and Chelubai.
verses.explainer: The sons of Hezron: Jerahmeel, Ram, and Chelubai -- three important branches of the Judahite family.

verses.text: Ram became the father of Amminadab, and Amminadab became the father of Nahshon, prince of the children of Judah;
verses.explainer: Ram fathered Amminadab, and Amminadab fathered Nahshon, prince of the sons of Judah -- the direct line from Hezron to David begins.

verses.text: and Nahshon became the father of Salma, and Salma became the father of Boaz,
verses.explainer: Nahshon fathered Salma, and Salma fathered Boaz -- the line moves through the period of Judges.

verses.text: and Boaz became the father of Obed, and Obed became the father of Jesse;
verses.explainer: Boaz fathered Obed, and Obed fathered Jesse -- the connection through Ruth's family, linking the Davidic line to the story of Gentile inclusion.

crossReferences.connection: The genealogy at the end of Ruth, which this passage echoes, tracing the line from Perez to David

verses.text: and Jesse became the father of his firstborn Eliab, and Abinadab the second, and Shimea the third,
verses.explainer: Jesse fathered Eliab his firstborn, Abinadab, Shimea -- David's older brothers, passed over when God chose David.

crossReferences.connection: The account of Samuel anointing David over his older brothers

verses.text: Nethanel the fourth, Raddai the fifth,
verses.explainer: Nethanel, Raddai -- more of Jesse's sons.

verses.text: Ozem the sixth, David the seventh;
verses.explainer: Ozem, and David -- David is listed as the seventh son, a number of completeness and divine perfection.

verses.text: and their sisters were Zeruiah and Abigail. The sons of Zeruiah: Abishai, Joab, and Asahel, three.
verses.explainer: Their sisters were Zeruiah and Abigail, and Zeruiah had three sons: Abishai, Joab, and Asahel -- David's nephews who became his military leaders.

verses.text: Abigail bore Amasa; and the father of Amasa was Jether the Ishmaelite.
verses.explainer: Abigail bore Amasa, whose father was Jether the Ishmaelite -- Amasa later served as Absalom's commander, showing how family connections shaped political alignments.

verses.text: Caleb the son of Hezron became the father of children of Azubah his wife, and of Jerioth; and these were her sons: Jesher, Shobab, and Ardon.
verses.explainer: Caleb son of Hezron had children by his wife Azubah and by Jerioth -- the Calebite branch of the Judahite family.

verses.text: Azubah died, and Caleb married Ephrath, who bore him Hur.
verses.explainer: When Azubah died, Caleb married Ephrath, who bore Hur -- Hur's grandson Bezalel would build the tabernacle.

crossReferences.connection: Bezalel son of Uri son of Hur, the master craftsman of the tabernacle

verses.text: Hur became the father of Uri, and Uri became the father of Bezalel.
verses.explainer: Hur fathered Uri, and Uri fathered Bezalel -- the line leading to the tabernacle's builder, connecting worship craftsmanship to Judah's family.

verses.text: Afterward Hezron went in to the daughter of Machir the father of Gilead, whom he took as wife when he was sixty years old; and she bore him Segub.
verses.explainer: Hezron married Machir's daughter when he was sixty and she bore Segub -- an intermarriage with the Transjordanian tribe of Manasseh.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The intermarriage between Judahite and Manassite families shows the complex web of relationships between tribes that the genealogies preserve.

verses.text: Segub became the father of Jair, who had twenty-three cities in the land of Gilead.
verses.explainer: Segub fathered Jair, who had twenty-three cities in Gilead -- a Judahite with extensive Transjordanian holdings.

verses.text: Geshur and Aram took the towns of Jair from them, with Kenath, and its villages, even sixty cities. All these were the sons of Machir the father of Gilead.
verses.explainer: Geshur and Aram took the towns of Jair, Kenath, and its villages -- sixty towns lost to foreign conquest.

verses.text: After Hezron died in Caleb Ephrathah, Abijah Hezron’s wife bore him Ashhur the father of Tekoa.
verses.explainer: After Hezron died in Caleb-ephrathah, his wife Abijah bore Ashhur the father of Tekoa.

verses.text: The sons of Jerahmeel the firstborn of Hezron were Ram the firstborn, Bunah, Oren, Ozem, and Ahijah.
verses.explainer: The sons of Jerahmeel the firstborn of Hezron -- the Jerahmeelite branch of the Judahite family.

verses.text: Jerahmeel had another wife, whose name was Atarah. She was the mother of Onam.
verses.explainer: Jerahmeel had another wife named Atarah; she was the mother of Onam.

verses.text: The sons of Ram the firstborn of Jerahmeel were Maaz, Jamin, and Eker.
verses.explainer: The sons of Ram, the firstborn of Jerahmeel.

verses.text: The sons of Onam were Shammai and Jada. The sons of Shammai: Nadab and Abishur.
verses.explainer: The sons of Onam: Shammai and Jada.

verses.text: The name of the wife of Abishur was Abihail; and she bore him Ahban and Molid.
verses.explainer: Abishur's wife Abihail bore him Ahban and Molid.

verses.text: The sons of Nadab: Seled and Appaim; but Seled died without children.
verses.explainer: The sons of Nadab: Seled and Appaim. Seled died without children.

verses.text: The son of Appaim: Ishi. The son of Ishi: Sheshan. The son of Sheshan: Ahlai.
verses.explainer: Appaim's son Ishi, Ishi's son Sheshan.

verses.text: The sons of Jada the brother of Shammai: Jether and Jonathan; and Jether died without children.
verses.explainer: The sons of Jada: Jether and Jonathan. Jether died without children.

verses.text: The sons of Jonathan: Peleth and Zaza. These were the sons of Jerahmeel.
verses.explainer: The sons of Jonathan: Peleth and Zaza -- completing the Jerahmeelite branch.

verses.text: Now Sheshan had no sons, but daughters. Sheshan had a servant, an Egyptian, whose name was Jarha.
verses.explainer: Sheshan had no sons, only daughters, and gave his daughter in marriage to his Egyptian servant Jarha -- an unusual arrangement that preserved the family line.

thenVsNow.then: The inclusion of non-Israelites in Judah's genealogy (Tamar the Canaanite, Jarha the Egyptian) shows that Israel's identity was not purely ethnic but was open to those who joined the community.
thenVsNow.now: The inclusion of non-Israelites in Judah's genealogy (Tamar the Canaanite, Jarha the Egyptian) shows that Israel's identity was not purely ethnic but was open to those who joined the community.

verses.text: Sheshan gave his daughter to Jarha his servant as wife; and she bore him Attai.
verses.explainer: Sheshan gave his daughter to Jarha, and she bore Attai.

verses.text: Attai became the father of Nathan, and Nathan became the father of Zabad,
verses.explainer: Attai fathered Nathan, Nathan fathered Zabad.

verses.text: and Zabad became the father of Ephlal, and Ephlal became the father of Obed,
verses.explainer: Zabad fathered Ephlal, Ephlal fathered Obed.

verses.text: and Obed became the father of Jehu, and Jehu became the father of Azariah,
verses.explainer: Obed fathered Jehu, Jehu fathered Azariah.

verses.text: and Azariah became the father of Helez, and Helez became the father of Eleasah,
verses.explainer: Azariah fathered Helez, Helez fathered Eleasah.

verses.text: and Eleasah became the father of Sismai, and Sismai became the father of Shallum,
verses.explainer: Eleasah fathered Sismai, Sismai fathered Shallum.

verses.text: and Shallum became the father of Jekamiah, and Jekamiah became the father of Elishama.
verses.explainer: Shallum fathered Jekamiah, Jekamiah fathered Elishama -- a thirteen-generation chain from the Egyptian servant to the post-exilic period.

verses.text: The sons of Caleb the brother of Jerahmeel were Mesha his firstborn, who was the father of Ziph; and the sons of Mareshah the father of Hebron.
verses.explainer: The sons of Caleb, brother of Jerahmeel -- the Calebite clans and their territories.

verses.text: The sons of Hebron: Korah, Tappuah, Rekem, and Shema.
verses.explainer: The sons of Hebron: Korah, Tappuah, Rekem, and Shema.

verses.text: Shema became the father of Raham, the father of Jorkeam; and Rekem became the father of Shammai.
verses.explainer: Shema fathered Raham, and Rekem fathered Shammai.

verses.text: The son of Shammai was Maon; and Maon was the father of Beth Zur.
verses.explainer: Shammai's son Maon, and Maon was the father of Beth-zur -- the city of Beth-zur connected to its founding family.

verses.text: Ephah, Caleb’s concubine, bore Haran, Moza, and Gazez; and Haran became the father of Gazez.
verses.explainer: Ephah, Caleb's concubine, bore Haran, Moza, and Gazez.

verses.text: The sons of Jahdai: Regem, Jothan, Geshan, Pelet, Ephah, and Shaaph.
verses.explainer: The sons of Jahdai.

verses.text: Maacah, Caleb’s concubine, bore Sheber and Tirhanah.
verses.explainer: Maacah, Caleb's concubine, bore Sheber and Tirhanah.

verses.text: She bore also Shaaph the father of Madmannah, Sheva the father of Machbena, and the father of Gibea; and the daughter of Caleb was Achsah.
verses.explainer: She also bore Shaaph father of Madmannah, Sheva father of Machbenah and Gibea.

verses.text: These were the sons of Caleb, the son of Hur, the firstborn of Ephrathah: Shobal the father of Kiriath Jearim,
verses.explainer: These were the descendants of Caleb. The sons of Hur, the firstborn of Ephrathah: Shobal father of Kiriath-jearim -- connecting Calebite families to known Judahite cities.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: These genealogies map families to specific cities and territories, establishing land claims and civic identity for the post-exilic community. Knowing which family founded which city was essential for property restoration after the return.

verses.text: Salma the father of Bethlehem, and Hareph the father of Beth Gader.
verses.explainer: Salma father of Bethlehem, Hareph father of Beth-gader -- Bethlehem's founding family traced to Judah's line.

verses.text: Shobal the father of Kiriath Jearim had sons: Haroeh, half of the Menuhoth.
verses.explainer: Shobal the father of Kiriath-jearim had sons -- Kiriath-jearim was where the ark rested for twenty years.

verses.text: The families of Kiriath Jearim: the Ithrites, the Puthites, the Shumathites, and the Mishraites; from them came the Zorathites and the Eshtaolites.
verses.explainer: The clans of Kiriath-jearim -- the families associated with this important city.

verses.text: The sons of Salma: Bethlehem, the Netophathites, Atroth Beth Joab, and half of the Manahathites, the Zorites.
verses.explainer: The sons of Salma: Bethlehem, the Netophathites, Atroth-beth-joab, half of the Manahathites, the Zorites -- Salma's descendants populated Bethlehem and surrounding areas.

verses.text: The families of scribes who lived at Jabez: the Tirathites, the Shimeathites, and the Sucathites. These are the Kenites who came from Hammath, the father of the house of Rechab.
verses.explainer: The clans of scribes at Jabez: Tirathites, Shimeathites, Sucathites. These are the Kenites who came from Hammath, father of the house of Rechab -- scribal families connected to the Kenite tradition.

explainer.summary: The genealogy of David's royal descendants, listing his sons born in Hebron and Jerusalem, then tracing the Davidic line through Solomon to the exile and beyond. This chapter is one of the most theologically important genealogies in the Old Testament, demonstrating that the Davidic line survived the exile.
themes: The Davidic royal line through exile
The covenant promise continuing beyond catastrophe
The genealogical bridge from Solomon to the post-exilic community
explainer.contextInBook: This chapter provides the crucial evidence that the Davidic covenant survived the exile. For the returned community, knowing that David's descendants were identifiable and alive was the basis for messianic hope.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Another name for Jehoiachin, the exiled king whose
wordStudies.meaning: Another name for Jehoiachin, the exiled king whose release from prison ends 2 Kings. His appearance in this genealogy shows the Davidic line continuing through exile -- the covenant promise is not dead, merely in exile.

verses.text: Now these were the sons of David, who were born to him in Hebron: the firstborn, Amnon, of Ahinoam the Jezreelitess; the second, Daniel, of Abigail the Carmelitess;
verses.explainer: The sons of David born in Hebron: Amnon the firstborn by Ahinoam, Daniel by Abigail -- David's family during his seven-year reign in Hebron.

verses.text: the third, Absalom the son of Maacah the daughter of Talmai king of Geshur; the fourth, Adonijah the son of Haggith;
verses.explainer: The third Absalom son of Maacah princess of Geshur, the fourth Adonijah son of Haggith -- the sons who will later compete for the throne.

verses.text: the fifth, Shephatiah of Abital; the sixth, Ithream by Eglah his wife:
verses.explainer: The fifth Shephatiah by Abital, the sixth Ithream by Eglah -- six sons in Hebron, representing six different wives.

verses.text: six were born to him in Hebron; and he reigned there seven years and six months. He reigned thirty-three years in Jerusalem;
verses.explainer: Six were born in Hebron where he reigned seven years and six months, and in Jerusalem he reigned thirty-three years -- the summary dividing David's reign into two phases.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: David's multiple wives were standard for ancient Near Eastern kings, reflecting political alliances. Each wife represented a relationship with her family's clan or region.

verses.text: and these were born to him in Jerusalem: Shimea, Shobab, Nathan, and Solomon, four, of Bathshua the daughter of Ammiel;
verses.explainer: The sons born in Jerusalem: Shimea, Shobab, Nathan, and Solomon, four by Bathshua daughter of Ammiel -- Bathsheba (here called Bath-shua) and her four sons, including the heir Solomon.

crossReferences.connection: Luke's genealogy of Jesus traces through Nathan rather than Solomon, using this same David-to-Nathan line

verses.text: and Ibhar, Elishama, Eliphelet,
verses.explainer: Ibhar, Elishama, Eliphelet -- more sons born in Jerusalem.

verses.text: Nogah, Nepheg, Japhia,
verses.explainer: Nogah, Nepheg, Japhia -- additional sons.

verses.text: Elishama, Eliada, and Eliphelet, nine.
verses.explainer: Elishama, Eliada, and Eliphelet, nine -- nine sons in Jerusalem plus the four by Bathsheba makes thirteen.

verses.text: All these were the sons of David, besides the sons of the concubines; and Tamar was their sister.
verses.explainer: All the sons of David besides the sons of the concubines, and Tamar was their sister -- the complete roster of David's named children, with Tamar specifically mentioned.

verses.text: Solomon’s son was Rehoboam, Abijah his son, Asa his son, Jehoshaphat his son,
verses.explainer: Solomon's son Rehoboam, his son Abijah, his son Asa, his son Jehoshaphat -- the Davidic succession from Solomon through the good and bad kings of Judah.

thenVsNow.then: This rapid succession list connects David to the post-exilic community through an unbroken chain of fathers and sons, proving that the Davidic covenant survived centuries of good and bad kings.
thenVsNow.now: This rapid succession list connects David to the post-exilic community through an unbroken chain of fathers and sons, proving that the Davidic covenant survived centuries of good and bad kings.

verses.text: Joram his son, Ahaziah his son, Joash his son,
verses.explainer: His son Joram, his son Ahaziah, his son Joash -- the line continuing through the monarchic period.

verses.text: Amaziah his son, Azariah his son, Jotham his son,
verses.explainer: His son Amaziah, his son Azariah, his son Jotham -- the mid-period kings of Judah.

verses.text: Ahaz his son, Hezekiah his son, Manasseh his son,
verses.explainer: His son Ahaz, his son Hezekiah, his son Manasseh -- from apostasy through reform to the worst king.

verses.text: Amon his son, and Josiah his son.
verses.explainer: His son Amon, his son Josiah -- the final good king before the collapse.

verses.text: The sons of Josiah: the firstborn Johanan, the second Jehoiakim, the third Zedekiah, and the fourth Shallum.
verses.explainer: The sons of Josiah: Johanan the firstborn, Jehoiakim, Zedekiah, Shallum -- the last generation of kings, all of whom reigned briefly before exile.

verses.text: The sons of Jehoiakim: Jeconiah his son, and Zedekiah his son.
verses.explainer: The sons of Jehoiakim: Jeconiah and Zedekiah -- Jeconiah (Jehoiachin) was exiled to Babylon, carrying the Davidic line into exile.

crossReferences.connection: Jehoiachin's release from prison in Babylon, showing the Davidic line alive in exile
Jeconiah appears in Jesus' genealogy, connecting the exilic Davidic line to the Messiah

verses.text: The sons of Jeconiah, the captive: Shealtiel his son,
verses.explainer: The sons of Jeconiah the captive: Shealtiel -- the Davidic line continues in Babylon through the exiled king.

verses.text: Malchiram, Pedaiah, Shenazzar, Jekamiah, Hoshama, and Nedabiah.
verses.explainer: Malchiram, Pedaiah, Shenazzar, Jecamiah, Hoshama, and Nedabiah -- six sons of the exiled king, ensuring the line's continuation in Babylon.

verses.text: The sons of Pedaiah: Zerubbabel and Shimei. The sons of Zerubbabel: Meshullam and Hananiah; and Shelomith was their sister;
verses.explainer: The sons of Pedaiah: Zerubbabel and Shimei. Zerubbabel's sons -- Zerubbabel, the leader of the return, is the crucial link between exile and restoration.

crossReferences.connection: Zerubbabel leading the return and rebuilding the temple
God calls Zerubbabel His 'signet ring,' reversing the curse on Jehoiachin's line

verses.text: and Hashubah, Ohel, Berechiah, Hasadiah, and Jushab Hesed, five.
verses.explainer: The sons of Meshullam and Hananiah, and Shelomith was their sister.

verses.text: The sons of Hananiah: Pelatiah and Jeshaiah; the sons of Rephaiah, the sons of Arnan, the sons of Obadiah, the sons of Shecaniah.
verses.explainer: The sons of Hananiah: Pelatiah and Jeshaiah, the sons of Rephaiah, Arnan, Obadiah, Shecaniah -- the Davidic line continuing into the post-exilic generations.

thenVsNow.then: These names represent the post-exilic Davidic family -- proof that the covenant line survived the greatest catastrophe in Israel's history and continued into the period of the returned community.
thenVsNow.now: These names represent the post-exilic Davidic family -- proof that the covenant line survived the greatest catastrophe in Israel's history and continued into the period of the returned community.

verses.text: The son of Shecaniah: Shemaiah. The sons of Shemaiah: Hattush, Igal, Bariah, Neariah, and Shaphat, six.
verses.explainer: The son of Shecaniah: Shemaiah. And the sons of Shemaiah -- the genealogy extends well into the post-exilic period.

verses.text: The sons of Neariah: Elioenai, Hizkiah, and Azrikam, three.
verses.explainer: The sons of Neariah: Elioenai, Hizkiah, Azrikam, three.

verses.text: The sons of Elioenai: Hodaviah, Eliashib, Pelaiah, Akkub, Johanan, Delaiah, and Anani, seven.
verses.explainer: The sons of Elioenai: Hodaviah, Eliashib, Pelaiah, Akkub, Johanan, Delaiah, Anani -- seven sons in the final generation, extending the Davidic line to approximately the Chronicler's own time.

explainer.summary: Additional genealogies of Judahite clans, including the famous prayer of Jabez, followed by the genealogy of the tribe of Simeon. These records establish the family connections and territorial claims of specific clans within Judah's territory, information essential for the post-exilic restoration of property and civic identity.
themes: Clan and city connections in Judah's territory
The prayer of Jabez: seeking God's blessing
Occupational and territorial identities of families
explainer.contextInBook: These genealogies continue mapping Judahite families to specific cities and occupations. The prayer of Jabez stands out as the one personal prayer within nine chapters of genealogies, highlighting the importance of seeking God's blessing in the midst of establishing identity.

wordStudies.meaning: Means 'he causes pain.' His mother named him for the pain of his birth. Jabez prays to reverse the destiny his name implies, asking God to bless him, expand his territory, and keep him from harm. God grants the request -- showing that names and circumstances do not determine destiny.

To bless, kneel, or praise. Jabez asks God to 'bless me indeed' -- an emphatic request for divine favor. The Chronicler includes this prayer in the middle of genealogies to show that personal relationship with God matters as much as genealogical identity.

verses.text: The sons of Judah: Perez, Hezron, Carmi, Hur, and Shobal.
verses.explainer: The sons of Judah: Perez, Hezron, Carmi, Hur, and Shobal -- a summary list introducing additional Judahite clans.

verses.text: Reaiah the son of Shobal became the father of Jahath; and Jahath became the father of Ahumai and Lahad. These are the families of the Zorathites.
verses.explainer: Reaiah son of Shobal fathered Jahath, and Jahath fathered Ahumai and Lahad. These are the clans of the Zorathites.

verses.text: These were the sons of the father of Etam: Jezreel, Ishma, and Idbash. The name of their sister was Hazzelelponi.
verses.explainer: These are the sons of Etam: Jezreel, Ishma, and Idbash; their sister's name was Hazzelelponi.

verses.text: Penuel was the father of Gedor and Ezer the father of Hushah. These are the sons of Hur, the firstborn of Ephrathah, the father of Bethlehem.
verses.explainer: Penuel was the father of Gedor, and Ezer was the father of Hushah -- clans connected to specific towns.

verses.text: Ashhur the father of Tekoa had two wives, Helah and Naarah.
verses.explainer: Ashhur, the father of Tekoa, had two wives: Helah and Naarah.

verses.text: Naarah bore him Ahuzzam, Hepher, Temeni, and Haahashtari. These were the sons of Naarah.
verses.explainer: Naarah bore him Ahuzzam, Hepher, Temeni, and Haahashtari.

verses.text: The sons of Helah were Zereth, Izhar, and Ethnan.
verses.explainer: The sons of Helah: Zereth, Izhar, and Ethnan.

verses.text: Hakkoz became the father of Anub, Zobebah, and the families of Aharhel the son of Harum.
verses.explainer: Koz fathered Anub, Zobebah, and the clans of Aharhel son of Harum.

verses.text: Jabez was more honorable than his brothers. His mother named him Jabez, saying, “Because I bore him with sorrow.”
verses.explainer: Jabez was more honorable than his brothers, and his mother named him Jabez because she bore him in pain -- the only biographical detail in these genealogies, highlighting a man who transcended his painful origins through prayer.

verses.text:
Jabez called on the God of Israel, saying, “Oh that you would bless me indeed, and enlarge my border! May your hand be with me, and may you keep me from evil, that I may not cause pain!”
God granted him that which he requested.
verses.explainer: Jabez called on the God of Israel: 'Oh that you would bless me and enlarge my border, and that your hand might be with me, and that you would keep me from harm so that it might not bring me pain!' And God granted what he asked.

annotations.word: Oh that you would bless me indeed

thenVsNow.then: The prayer of Jabez illustrates that one's starting point -- painful birth, unfortunate name, difficult circumstances -- does not determine one's destiny. Prayer to the God of Israel can transform the trajectory of a life.
thenVsNow.now: The prayer of Jabez illustrates that one's starting point -- painful birth, unfortunate name, difficult circumstances -- does not determine one's destiny. Prayer to the God of Israel can transform the trajectory of a life.

verses.text: Chelub the brother of Shuhah became the father of Mehir, who was the father of Eshton.
verses.explainer: Chelub, Shuhah's brother, fathered Mehir, who fathered Eshton.

verses.text: Eshton became the father of Beth Rapha, Paseah, and Tehinnah the father of Ir Nahash. These are the men of Recah.
verses.explainer: Eshton fathered Beth-rapha, Paseah, and Tehinnah the father of Ir-nahash -- clans connected to their founding cities.

verses.text: The sons of Kenaz: Othniel and Seraiah. The sons of Othniel: Hathath.
verses.explainer: The sons of Kenaz: Othniel and Seraiah. Othniel's sons: Hathath and Meonothai.

verses.text: Meonothai became the father of Ophrah: and Seraiah became the father of Joab the father of Ge Harashim; for they were craftsmen.
verses.explainer: Meonothai fathered Ophrah, and Seraiah fathered Joab the father of Ge-harashim, 'the Valley of Craftsmen,' for they were craftsmen.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The 'Valley of Craftsmen' shows that occupational guilds were organized by family. Skilled crafts -- metalworking, weaving, pottery -- were passed down within specific families, making these genealogies also occupational records.

verses.text: The sons of Caleb the son of Jephunneh: Iru, Elah, and Naam. The son of Elah: Kenaz.
verses.explainer: The sons of Caleb son of Jephunneh: Iru, Elah, and Naam.

verses.text: The sons of Jehallelel: Ziph, Ziphah, Tiria, and Asarel.
verses.explainer: The sons of Jehallelel.

verses.text: The sons of Ezrah: Jether, Mered, Epher, and Jalon; and she bore Miriam, Shammai, and Ishbah the father of Eshtemoa.
verses.explainer: The sons of Ezrah.

verses.text: His wife the Jewess bore Jered the father of Gedor, Heber the father of Soco, and Jekuthiel the father of Zanoah. These are the sons of Bithiah the daughter of Pharaoh, whom Mered took.
verses.explainer: His Judahite wife bore Jered father of Gedor, Heber father of Soco, and Jekuthiel father of Zanoah. These are the sons of Bithiah daughter of Pharaoh, whom Mered married.

verses.text: The sons of the wife of Hodiah, the sister of Naham, were the father of Keilah the Garmite, and Eshtemoa the Maacathite.
verses.explainer: The sons of the wife of Hodiah, the sister of Naham -- the Keilah and Maacathite clans.

verses.text: The sons of Shimon: Amnon, Rinnah, Ben Hanan, and Tilon. The sons of Ishi: Zoheth, and Ben Zoheth.
verses.explainer: The sons of Shimon.

verses.text: The sons of Shelah the son of Judah: Er the father of Lecah, Laadah the father of Mareshah, and the families of the house of those who worked fine linen, of the house of Ashbea;
verses.explainer: The sons of Shelah son of Judah: Er father of Lecah, Laadah father of Mareshah, and the clans of linen workers at Beth-ashbea.

verses.text: and Jokim, and the men of Cozeba, and Joash, and Saraph, who had dominion in Moab, and Jashubilehem. These records are ancient.
verses.explainer: Jokim, the men of Cozeba, Joash, and Saraph who ruled in Moab and returned to Bethlehem. These are ancient records.

verses.text: These were the potters, and the inhabitants of Netaim and Gederah: they lived there with the king for his work.
verses.explainer: These were the potters and inhabitants of Netaim and Gederah; they lived there with the king for his work -- royal craftsmen who served the monarchy.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Royal potters and craftsmen lived in designated settlements near the king's estates. Archaeological evidence confirms royal stamp marks on pottery from specific production centers in Judah.

verses.text: The sons of Simeon: Nemuel, Jamin, Jarib, Zerah, Shaul;
verses.explainer: The sons of Simeon: Nemuel, Jamin, Jarib, Zerah, Shaul -- the genealogy shifts to Simeon, the tribe absorbed into Judah's territory.

verses.text: Shallum his son, Mibsam his son, and Mishma his son.
verses.explainer: Shallum his son, Mibsam his son, Mishma his son.

verses.text: The sons of Mishma: Hammuel his son, Zaccur his son, Shimei his son.
verses.explainer: The sons of Mishma: Hammuel, Zaccur, Shimei.

verses.text: Shimei had sixteen sons and six daughters; but his brothers didn’t have many children, and all their family did not multiply like the children of Judah.
verses.explainer: Shimei had sixteen sons and six daughters, but his brothers did not have many children, so their clan did not multiply like Judah -- explaining Simeon's demographic decline.

thenVsNow.then: The note about Simeon not multiplying like Judah explains the tribe's gradual disappearance as a distinct entity -- a demographic reality reflected in the genealogical record.
thenVsNow.now: The note about Simeon not multiplying like Judah explains the tribe's gradual disappearance as a distinct entity -- a demographic reality reflected in the genealogical record.

verses.text: They lived at Beersheba, Moladah, Hazarshual,
verses.explainer: They lived in Beersheba, Moladah, Hazar-shual -- Simeonite cities in the Negev, the southern desert region.

verses.text: at Bilhah, at Ezem, at Tolad,
verses.explainer: Bilhah, Ezem, Tolad -- more Simeonite settlements.

verses.text: at Bethuel, at Hormah, at Ziklag,
verses.explainer: Bethuel, Hormah, Ziklag -- cities also associated with David's early career as a fugitive.

verses.text: at Beth Marcaboth, Hazar Susim, at Beth Biri, and at Shaaraim. These were their cities until David’s reign.
verses.explainer: Beth-marcaboth, Hazar-susim, Beth-biri, and Shaaraim -- Simeonite territories that were eventually absorbed into Judah.

verses.text: Their villages were Etam, Ain, Rimmon, Tochen, and Ashan, five cities;
verses.explainer: Their villages included Etam, Ain, Rimmon, Tochen, and Ashan -- five satellite villages.

verses.text: and all their villages that were around the same cities, to Baal. These were their settlements, and they have their genealogy.
verses.explainer: All their settlements around these towns as far as Baal. These were their habitations and their genealogical registrations.

verses.text: Meshobab, Jamlech, Joshah the son of Amaziah,
verses.explainer: Meshobab, Jamlech, Joshah -- Simeonite clan leaders.

verses.text: Joel, Jehu the son of Joshibiah, the son of Seraiah, the son of Asiel,
verses.explainer: Joel and Jehu son of Joshibiah -- more leaders.

verses.text: Elioenai, Jaakobah, Jeshohaiah, Asaiah, Adiel, Jesimiel, Benaiah,
verses.explainer: Elioenai, Jaakobah, Jeshohaiah, Asaiah, Adiel, Jesimiel, Benaiah -- a substantial list of clan leaders.

verses.text: and Ziza the son of Shiphi, the son of Allon, the son of Jedaiah, the son of Shimri, the son of Shemaiah—
verses.explainer: Ziza son of Shiphi -- another leader.

verses.text: these mentioned by name were princes in their families. Their fathers’ houses increased greatly.
verses.explainer: These mentioned by name were princes in their clans, and their families increased greatly -- Simeonite leaders whose clans expanded.

verses.text: They went to the entrance of Gedor, even to the east side of the valley, to seek pasture for their flocks.
verses.explainer: They went to the entrance of Gedor, east of the valley, seeking pasture for their flocks -- Simeonite expansion into new territory for grazing.

verses.text: They found fat pasture and good, and the land was wide, and quiet, and peaceful; for those who lived there before were descended from Ham.
verses.explainer: They found rich, good pasture, and the land was broad, quiet, and peaceful, for former Hamite inhabitants had lived there -- discovering productive land with sparse population.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The Simeonite expansion into areas formerly held by Hamite peoples shows the gradual process by which Israelite tribes occupied the land -- not a single conquest but ongoing settlement over generations.

verses.text: These written by name came in the days of Hezekiah king of Judah, and struck their tents. The Meunim who were found there, and they destroyed them utterly to this day, and lived in their place; because there was pasture there for their flocks.
verses.explainer: These listed by name came in the days of Hezekiah king of Judah and attacked the Hamite settlements and the Meunites there, devoting them to destruction -- Simeonite military expansion during Hezekiah's reign.

verses.text: Some of them, even of the sons of Simeon, five hundred men, went to Mount Seir, having for their captains Pelatiah, Neariah, Rephaiah, and Uzziel, the sons of Ishi.
verses.explainer: Five hundred Simeonites went to Mount Seir, led by Pelatiah, Neariah, Rephaiah, and Uzziel -- Simeonite expansion into Edomite territory.

verses.text: They struck the remnant of the Amalekites who escaped, and have lived there to this day.
verses.explainer: They struck the remaining Amalekites and have lived there to this day -- the Simeonites fulfilled part of the command to destroy the Amalekites.

crossReferences.connection: God's declaration of perpetual war against Amalek, partially fulfilled here by Simeonite warriors

explainer.summary: Chapter 5 traces the tribes of Reuben, Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh who settled east of the Jordan. It records their victories when they trusted God and their exile when they turned to foreign gods, providing a compact theological lesson about faithfulness and consequences.
explainer.contextInBook: After Judah and Simeon's genealogies, the Chronicler turns to the eastern tribes, showing that even tribes outside the Promised Land's heartland were part of God's people -- but also illustrating the pattern of faithfulness leading to blessing and unfaithfulness leading to exile.

wordStudies.translatedAs: The rights and double inheritance portion belongin
wordStudies.meaning: The rights and double inheritance portion belonging to the firstborn son. Reuben lost his birthright due to his sin, showing that covenant standing depends on faithfulness, not mere birth order.

wordStudies.translatedAs: To uncover, remove, or go into exile
wordStudies.meaning: To uncover, remove, or go into exile. Used for the Assyrian deportation of the eastern tribes, this word emphasizes the stripping away of everything -- land, identity, and worship -- as consequence of covenant unfaithfulness.

verses.text: The sons of Reuben the firstborn of Israel (for he was the firstborn; but, because he defiled his father’s couch, his birthright was given to the sons of Joseph the son of Israel; and the genealogy is not to be listed according to the birthright.
verses.explainer: Reuben was Jacob's firstborn but lost his birthright because he defiled his father's bed -- a reminder that covenant privilege depends on faithfulness, not mere birth order.

crossReferences.connection: The original account of Reuben's sin with Bilhah
Jacob's deathbed declaration stripping Reuben of his preeminence

verses.text: For Judah prevailed above his brothers, and from him came the prince; but the birthright was Joseph’s)—
verses.explainer: Judah prevailed over his brothers and from him came the prince, yet the birthright belonged to Joseph -- a complex redistribution of firstborn privileges among the tribes.

verses.text: the sons of Reuben the firstborn of Israel: Hanoch, Pallu, Hezron, and Carmi.
verses.explainer: The sons of Reuben are listed beginning with Hanoch, establishing the tribe's lineage despite its loss of firstborn status.

thenVsNow.then: Losing privilege due to moral failure is a universal principle -- in families, organizations, and communities, trust once broken changes the inheritance of responsibility.
thenVsNow.now: Losing privilege due to moral failure is a universal principle -- in families, organizations, and communities, trust once broken changes the inheritance of responsibility.

verses.text: The sons of Joel: Shemaiah his son, Gog his son, Shimei his son,
verses.explainer: The genealogy continues through Joel's line, tracing Reuben's descendants through multiple generations.

verses.text: Micah his son, Reaiah his son, Baal his son,
verses.explainer: Micah and his descendants continue the Reubenite line, maintaining the tribal record.

verses.text: and Beerah his son, whom Tilgath Pilneser king of Assyria carried away captive. He was prince of the Reubenites.
verses.explainer: Beerah was a Reubenite chief whom Tiglath-Pileser of Assyria carried into exile, connecting this genealogy directly to the historical tragedy of deportation.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Assyrian deportation policy involved relocating conquered populations to break their national identity and prevent rebellion.

verses.text: His brothers by their families, when the genealogy of their generations was listed: the chief, Jeiel, and Zechariah,
verses.explainer: The relatives of Beerah are recorded by their genealogical registrations, with Jeiel as chief.

verses.text: and Bela the son of Azaz, the son of Shema, the son of Joel, who lived in Aroer, even to Nebo and Baal Meon;
verses.explainer: Bela son of Azaz settled in the territory stretching toward the Euphrates, indicating the extent of Reubenite settlement in prosperous times.

verses.text: and he lived eastward even to the entrance of the wilderness from the river Euphrates, because their livestock were multiplied in the land of Gilead.
verses.explainer: The Reubenites expanded eastward because their cattle had multiplied in Gilead -- prosperity drove territorial expansion.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Pastoral expansion was a primary driver of territorial growth in the ancient Near East, as large herds required ever more grazing land.

verses.text: In the days of Saul, they made war with the Hagrites, who fell by their hand; and they lived in their tents throughout all the land east of Gilead.
verses.explainer: In Saul's time, the Reubenites defeated the Hagrites and occupied their territory, showing the tribe's military capability when faithful.

verses.text: The sons of Gad lived over against them, in the land of Bashan to Salecah:
verses.explainer: The Gadites are introduced, living opposite the Reubenites in Bashan, establishing the second Transjordan tribe's genealogy.

verses.text: Joel the chief, Shapham the second, Janai, and Shaphat in Bashan.
verses.explainer: Joel was chief among the Gadites, with Shapham second, showing the tribe's organized leadership structure in Bashan.

verses.text: Their brothers of their fathers’ houses: Michael, Meshullam, Sheba, Jorai, Jacan, Zia, and Eber, seven.
verses.explainer: Seven clans of Gad are listed, documenting the tribe's breadth and organization.

verses.text: These were the sons of Abihail, the son of Huri, the son of Jaroah, the son of Gilead, the son of Michael, the son of Jeshishai, the son of Jahdo, the son of Buz;
verses.explainer: The Gadite genealogy traces through Abihail's line back through multiple generations, establishing ancestral credentials.

verses.text: Ahi the son of Abdiel, the son of Guni, chief of their fathers’ houses.
verses.explainer: Ahi son of Abdiel was head of the Gadite clan, recorded in the tribal registers.

verses.text: They lived in Gilead in Bashan, and in its towns, and in all the suburbs of Sharon, as far as their borders.
verses.explainer: The Gadites settled in Gilead, Bashan, and surrounding pasturelands -- a wide territory reflecting God's provision.

verses.text: All these were listed by genealogies in the days of Jotham king of Judah, and in the days of Jeroboam king of Israel.
verses.explainer: These genealogical records were compiled during the reigns of Jotham of Judah and Jeroboam of Israel, anchoring the genealogy in specific historical periods.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The mention of both a Judahite and Israelite king suggests this record predates the northern kingdom's fall, preserving pre-exile tribal organization.

verses.text: The sons of Reuben, the Gadites, and the half-tribe of Manasseh, of valiant men, men able to bear buckler and sword, and to shoot with bow, and skillful in war, were forty-four thousand seven hundred sixty, that were able to go out to war.
verses.explainer: The eastern tribes fielded 44,760 warriors -- men trained for battle with shield, sword, and bow -- a formidable military force.

thenVsNow.then: Communities achieve remarkable things when they unite around shared purpose. The combined strength of these tribes far exceeded what any single tribe could muster alone.
thenVsNow.now: Communities achieve remarkable things when they unite around shared purpose. The combined strength of these tribes far exceeded what any single tribe could muster alone.

verses.text: They made war with the Hagrites, with Jetur, and Naphish, and Nodab.
verses.explainer: They waged war against the Hagrites, Jetur, Naphish, and Nodab -- peoples competing for the same territory east of the Jordan.

verses.text: They were helped against them, and the Hagrites were delivered into their hand, and all who were with them; for they cried to God in the battle, and he answered them, because they put their trust in him.
verses.explainer: They received divine help against these enemies because they cried out to God during the battle and trusted in Him -- the theological key to the entire passage.

crossReferences.connection: Asa's prayer in battle follows the same pattern: crying out to God and receiving help

thenVsNow.then: The principle is timeless: genuine trust in God during crisis -- not mere religious formality -- opens the door to divine help.
thenVsNow.now: The principle is timeless: genuine trust in God during crisis -- not mere religious formality -- opens the door to divine help.

verses.text: They took away their livestock; of their camels fifty thousand, and of sheep two hundred fifty thousand, and of donkeys two thousand, and of men one hundred thousand.
verses.explainer: They captured enormous plunder: 50,000 camels, 250,000 sheep, 2,000 donkeys, and 100,000 captives -- staggering numbers showing the scale of victory when God fought for them.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: These numbers represent extraordinary wealth in a pastoral economy. Camels alone were extremely valuable, used for long-distance trade across desert routes.

verses.text: For many fell slain, because the war was of God. They lived in their place until the captivity.
verses.explainer: Many fell in battle because the war was from God, and the eastern tribes occupied the territory until the exile -- a compressed summary spanning centuries.

verses.text: The children of the half-tribe of Manasseh lived in the land: they increased from Bashan to Baal Hermon, Senir, and Mount Hermon.
verses.explainer: The half-tribe of Manasseh is introduced, settling from Bashan to Baal-Hermon and Mount Hermon -- the northernmost Israelite territory.

verses.text: These were the heads of their fathers’ houses: even Epher, Ishi, Eliel, Azriel, Jeremiah, Hodaviah, and Jahdiel, mighty men of valor, famous men, heads of their fathers’ houses.
verses.explainer: Their clan heads are listed as mighty warriors and famous men -- a tribe of military distinction.

verses.text: They trespassed against the God of their fathers, and played the prostitute after the gods of the peoples of the land, whom God destroyed before them.
verses.explainer: But they were unfaithful to God and prostituted themselves to the gods of the peoples God had destroyed -- the tragic turning point of the entire chapter.

crossReferences.connection: God's explicit warning against adopting the gods of conquered peoples

thenVsNow.then: Success and prosperity often carry the seeds of spiritual complacency. The very territory God gave them exposed them to the idols that would destroy them.
thenVsNow.now: Success and prosperity often carry the seeds of spiritual complacency. The very territory God gave them exposed them to the idols that would destroy them.

verses.text: So the God of Israel stirred up the spirit of Pul king of Assyria, and the spirit of Tilgath Pilneser king of Assyria, and he carried them away, even the Reubenites, and the Gadites, and the half-tribe of Manasseh, and brought them to Halah, Habor, Hara, and to the river of Gozan, to this day.
verses.explainer: So God stirred up the spirit of Tiglath-Pileser king of Assyria, who deported the eastern tribes to various locations in the Assyrian empire -- exile as direct consequence of unfaithfulness.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Assyrian deportation policy deliberately mixed populations to destroy national identities. The deported Israelites were scattered among foreign peoples and effectively lost to history -- the 'lost tribes' of Israel.

explainer.summary: Chapter 6 is the longest and most detailed genealogy in Chronicles, tracing the tribe of Levi through its three main branches (Gershom, Kohath, and Merari), the high-priestly line from Aaron to the exile, the temple musicians appointed by David, and the Levitical cities throughout Israel. This chapter is the theological heart of the genealogies because the Levites are central to the worship that defines Israel's identity.
themes: The priestly line from Aaron to the exile
Worship as the organizing principle of community life
explainer.contextInBook: Placed centrally in the genealogies, Levi's tribe receives by far the most attention because Chronicles is fundamentally about worship. The post-exilic community needed to establish the legitimacy of their priests and Levites, making this chapter practically essential for the restored temple's functioning.

wordStudies.translatedAs: To serve or minister, specifically in the context
wordStudies.meaning: To serve or minister, specifically in the context of sacred service before God. Distinguished from 'abad' (general labor), sharath implies privileged attendance in God's presence -- the Levites' unique calling.

wordStudies.translatedAs: To cover, make atonement, or reconcile
wordStudies.meaning: To cover, make atonement, or reconcile. Aaron and his descendants were appointed to make atonement for Israel, standing between a holy God and a sinful people. This mediatorial role foreshadows Christ's ultimate atonement.

wordStudies.translatedAs: The dwelling place -- God's portable sanctuary
wordStudies.meaning: The dwelling place -- God's portable sanctuary. From the root 'shakan' (to dwell), it emphasizes God's desire to live among His people, a theme that runs from the tabernacle through the temple to the incarnation.

verses.text: The sons of Levi: Gershon, Kohath, and Merari.
verses.explainer: The three sons of Levi -- Gershom, Kohath, and Merari -- establish the three main branches of the tribe that will serve God's sanctuary in different capacities.

verses.text: The sons of Kohath: Amram, Izhar, and Hebron, and Uzziel.
verses.explainer: Kohath's sons -- Amram, Izhar, Hebron, and Uzziel -- are listed, with Amram's line being the most significant as it produces Moses and Aaron.

crossReferences.connection: The original listing of Kohath's sons in the Exodus narrative

verses.text: The children of Amram: Aaron, Moses, and Miriam. The sons of Aaron: Nadab, Abihu, Eleazar, and Ithamar.
verses.explainer: The children of Amram are Aaron, Moses, and Miriam -- the three siblings who led Israel out of Egypt. Aaron's sons are also listed as the priestly line.

verses.text: Eleazar became the father of Phinehas, Phinehas became the father of Abishua,
verses.explainer: The high-priestly genealogy begins tracing from Eleazar through Phinehas, establishing the legitimate succession of Israel's chief priests.

verses.text: Abishua became the father of Bukki. Bukki became the father of Uzzi.
verses.explainer: The line continues through Abishua, connecting each generation of high priests in unbroken succession.

verses.text: Uzzi became the father of Zerahiah. Zerahiah became the father of Meraioth.
verses.explainer: Bukki and Uzzi continue the high-priestly line, maintaining the record of covenant priesthood.

verses.text: Meraioth became the father of Amariah. Amariah became the father of Ahitub.
verses.explainer: Zerahiah and Meraioth are listed in the ongoing priestly succession.

verses.text: Ahitub became the father of Zadok. Zadok became the father of Ahimaaz.
verses.explainer: Amariah and Ahitub continue the line, with Ahitub being a significant name recurring in the priestly records.

verses.text: Ahimaaz became the father of Azariah. Azariah became the father of Johanan.
verses.explainer: Zadok appears in the genealogy -- the priest who served David and Solomon and whose descendants became the dominant priestly family of the first temple.

crossReferences.connection: Zadok serving as priest alongside Abiathar during David's reign

verses.text: Johanan became the father of Azariah, who executed the priest’s office in the house that Solomon built in Jerusalem.
verses.explainer: Ahimaaz and Azariah continue the Zadokite priestly line into the temple period.

verses.text: Azariah became the father of Amariah. Amariah became the father of Ahitub.
verses.explainer: The priestly succession continues through Amariah and Ahitub, tracing the unbroken line through the monarchic period.

verses.text: Ahitub became the father of Zadok. Zadok became the father of Shallum.
verses.explainer: Zadok and Shallum appear in the continuing genealogy, showing the persistence of the priestly line across centuries.

verses.text: Shallum became the father of Hilkiah. Hilkiah became the father of Azariah.
verses.explainer: Hilkiah is listed -- likely the high priest who discovered the Book of the Law during Josiah's temple repairs, triggering the great reform.

crossReferences.connection: Hilkiah's discovery of the Book of the Law in the temple during Josiah's renovations

verses.text: Azariah became the father of Seraiah. Seraiah became the father of Jehozadak.
verses.explainer: Azariah and Seraiah continue the line, with Seraiah being the last high priest before the exile.

verses.text: Jehozadak went into captivity, when Yahweh carried Judah and Jerusalem away by the hand of Nebuchadnezzar.
verses.explainer: Jehozadak went into exile when God sent Judah and Jerusalem away by Nebuchadnezzar's hand -- the priestly line survived even deportation.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Jehozadak's son Joshua (Jeshua) would become the first high priest of the rebuilt temple, making this verse the crucial link between pre-exilic and post-exilic worship.

thenVsNow.then: Even when institutions collapse, God preserves a remnant to rebuild. The priestly line survived exile just as communities of faith survive catastrophic disruption.
thenVsNow.now: Even when institutions collapse, God preserves a remnant to rebuild. The priestly line survived exile just as communities of faith survive catastrophic disruption.

verses.text: The sons of Levi: Gershom, Kohath, and Merari.
verses.explainer: The three sons of Levi are listed again -- Gershom, Kohath, and Merari -- beginning a parallel genealogy that traces each branch's descendants for the purpose of temple service assignments.

verses.text: These are the names of the sons of Gershom: Libni and Shimei.
verses.explainer: The sons of Gershom are named, establishing the sub-clans of this Levitical branch.

verses.text: The sons of Kohath were Amram, Izhar, Hebron, and Uzziel.
verses.explainer: The sons of Kohath are listed again, connecting to the priestly and Levitical families that would serve in the temple.

crossReferences.connection: The Kohathite clans and their original tabernacle duties

verses.text: The sons of Merari: Mahli and Mushi. These are the families of the Levites according to their fathers’ households.
verses.explainer: The sons of Merari -- Mahli and Mushi -- complete the three-fold division of the Levitical tribe.

verses.text: Of Gershom: Libni his son, Jahath his son, Zimmah his son,
verses.explainer: The Gershomite genealogy is traced through Libni's line across multiple generations, establishing the family's historical depth.

verses.text: Joah his son, Iddo his son, Zerah his son, and Jeatherai his son.
verses.explainer: The line continues through Zimmah and Joah, maintaining the Gershomite record.

verses.text: The sons of Kohath: Amminadab his son, Korah his son, Assir his son,
verses.explainer: The Kohathite genealogy traces through Amminadab and Korah, with Korah being a significant ancestor of temple musicians.

verses.text: Elkanah his son, and Ebiasaph his son, Assir his son,
verses.explainer: Elkanah appears in the Kohathite line, continuing the genealogy toward the temple musicians.

verses.text: Tahath his son, Uriel his son, Uzziah his son, and Shaul his son.
verses.explainer: The line continues through Ebiasaph and Assir, tracing the Kohathite branch.

verses.text: The sons of Elkanah: Amasai and Ahimoth.
verses.explainer: Elkanah's descendants continue to be traced, building toward the identification of key temple worship leaders.

verses.text: As for Elkanah, the sons of Elkanah: Zophai his son, Nahath his son,
verses.explainer: Another Elkanah appears in the genealogy, showing how this name recurred across generations in the Levitical families.

verses.text: Eliab his son, Jeroham his son, and Elkanah his son.
verses.explainer: Eliab and Jeroham continue the line, approaching the era of Samuel.

verses.text: The sons of Samuel: the firstborn, Joel, and the second, Abijah.
verses.explainer: The sons of Samuel are listed -- Joel the firstborn and Abijah the second -- connecting the great prophet-judge to the Levitical genealogies.

crossReferences.connection: Samuel's birth narrative identifies him as an Ephraimite by location, but Chronicles reveals his Levitical ancestry

verses.text: The sons of Merari: Mahli, Libni his son, Shimei his son, Uzzah his son,
verses.explainer: The Merarite genealogy begins with Mahli and his descendants, completing the three-branch Levitical record.

verses.text: Shimea his son, Haggiah his son, Asaiah his son.
verses.explainer: The Merarite line continues through Shimea and Haggiah, maintaining the tribal record.

verses.text: These are they whom David set over the service of song in Yahweh’s house, after the ark came to rest there.
verses.explainer: Asaiah completes the Merarite line, and the passage transitions to David's organization of temple music.

verses.text: They ministered with song before the tabernacle of the Tent of Meeting, until Solomon had built Yahweh’s house in Jerusalem. They performed the duties of their office according to their order.
verses.explainer: David appointed musicians to minister with song before the tabernacle until Solomon built the temple, establishing organized worship as a permanent feature of Israelite life.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: David's innovation of organized musical worship was revolutionary. Previously, music accompanied specific events; David made it a continuous, structured ministry with professional musicians serving in rotation.

thenVsNow.then: David established that worship is not occasional but continuous -- a principle that shapes every community of faith that builds regular worship into its rhythm of life.
thenVsNow.now: David established that worship is not occasional but continuous -- a principle that shapes every community of faith that builds regular worship into its rhythm of life.

verses.text: These are those who served, and their sons. Of the sons of the Kohathites: Heman the singer, the son of Joel, the son of Samuel,
